reasoning: The informal hypothesis has denominators $(1-x)^{i+1}$ (one-minus-x raised to the power i+1, with explicit parentheses in the LaTeX), while the Lean code `1 - x ^ (i.1 + 1)` parses as $1 - x^{i+1}$ (since `^` binds tighter than `-`). These are mathematically distinct expressions. Concrete tuples show the hypothesis sets are neither contained in the other: e.g., for $n=1, x=1/2$, the tuple $(a_0,a_1)=(1,-1/2)$ satisfies the informal hypothesis but not the Lean one, while $(1,-3/2)$ satisfies the Lean hypothesis but not the informal one. Since the conclusions are identical but the hypothesis sets are incomparable, neither statement implies the other. (In fact, the informal statement as literally written with $(1-x)^{i+1}$ is false — a counterexample exists with $a_0=1,a_1=-1/2,x=1/2$ requiring $y=2\notin(0,1)$ — while the Lean encoding corresponds to the true Putnam 2013 A3 statement.)
